target: data/sections.json — HOL-REDUNDANCY: deduplicate eclipse/Chapman and aetheric refraction arguments
assessment: The curmudgeon identified 8 redundancy clusters (cycle 1, 2026-04-06). Since then, significant consolidation has already occurred: Section 7.11 correctly cross-references Section 1.5 for aetheric refraction; Section 6.7 already cross-references Section 4.2 for eclipse analysis. The remaining actionable work is narrower than the original scope suggested: (1) Section 6.7 point 2 still re-derives the eclipse non-discrimination argument in 3 sentences when 1 sentence + cross-ref would suffice; (2) the overall hub-and-spoke architecture is largely in place but underdocumented; (3) two spoke patches (WIN-010, SEC-6.2) are tracked separately as EXP-145 and EXP-146.

changes.description: Tighten point 2 (globe eclipse prediction) to use cross-reference instead of re-deriving the non-discrimination argument
changes.old_text_starts_with: 2. The globe also predicts eclipse magnetic effects. Solar eclipses produce measurable ionospheric conductivity changes
changes.old_text_ends_with: If both models predict the same result, the test is non-discriminating.
changes.replacement_html: 2. The globe also predicts eclipse magnetic effects. Standard ionospheric physics (the Chapman Sq mechanism, peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–30 nT eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations under conditions matching the dome's prediction window. See Section 4.2 for the full analysis. If both models predict the same result, the test is non-discriminating.
changes.rationale: Section 4.2 already contains the complete treatment including Chapman mechanism, Sq current explanation, coverage/local-time dependence, and literature citations. Re-deriving here creates maintenance burden — if the nT range is updated in 4.2, 6.7 would drift.

no_action_needed.reason: Section 7.11 already correctly implements hub-and-spoke: it opens with 'For a full analysis... see Section 1b' and focuses specifically on which WINs depend on aetheric refraction and why this makes them unfalsifiable. The 'three-jobs problem' is briefly restated (~1 paragraph) in 7.11 but this is appropriate — it's the minimum context needed for a reader who enters via the falsification tests tab. Section 1.5 (23,682 chars) remains the comprehensive hub; 7.11 (5,703 chars) is a focused spoke. The curmudgeon review was from 2026-04-06; this consolidation appears to have been addressed in subsequent edits.
Brief TLDR mention: 'Chapman mechanism, 5-25 nT' in a single summary sentence. This is the appropriate level for a tracking/kill-shot section — no re-derivation, no redundancy problem.

Five mentions across three conclusion sections, all brief inline references (e.g., 'Chapman 1933; Meza et al. 2021' as citations, or 'Chapman ionospheric mechanism' as a one-phrase explanation). Conclusions should be able to stand alone for readers who skip to them. These are not re-derivations — they are contextual references. No action needed.
Citation references ('Chapman (1933)') in bibliography section. Appropriate and necessary.

separate_exps_for_remaining_spokes.status: pending — will replace Chapman mechanism re-derivation with cross-ref to Section 4.2
pending — will tighten eclipse treatment in SEC-6.2 (currently has Chapman x2)

other_redundancy_clusters_not_in_scope: Relabeling standard physics (5 locations) — recommend separate EXP, lower priority due to genuine differentiation
Tidal WINs (5 near-identical entries) — recommend separate EXP for group restructuring
holes_addressed: HOL-REDUNDANCY major: Eclipse/Chapman hub designated (Section 4.2) with spoke patches for all 6+ locations now tracked or completed
HOL-REDUNDANCY major: Aetheric refraction confirmed already consolidated (Section 1.5 hub, Section 7.11 spoke)
HOL-REDUNDANCY: Section 6.7 point 2 tightened to remove re-derivation

anticipated_objections.objection: The Section 6.7 patch removes useful context for tab-skipping readers who only visit the synthesis tab.
anticipated_objections.response: The patch retains the key conclusion (5-30 nT, non-discriminating) and the cross-reference. A reader on the synthesis tab still gets the punchline. The full mechanism explanation is one click away in Section 4.2. This is the hub-and-spoke model working as intended.

anticipated_objections.objection: The aetheric refraction 'no action needed' assessment is premature — Section 7.11 still restates the three-jobs problem.
anticipated_objections.response: True. But the three-jobs summary in 7.11 is a single paragraph (versus ~4 pages in Section 1.5) and provides the minimum context needed for the falsification argument. A reader encountering 7.11 needs to know why aetheric refraction is unfalsifiable — the three jobs are the clearest demonstration. Removing it would force readers to follow a cross-reference just to understand the section's argument. The curmudgeon recommendation was to merge the 'core physics argument' into 1.5 — but 7.11 doesn't re-derive the physics (n(r) formula, coefficient derivation, sun/firmament collision). It restates only the conceptual problem. This is appropriate spoke-level detail.

anticipated_objections.objection: You should address all 8 redundancy clusters in one expansion, not defer 4 to separate EXPs.
anticipated_objections.response: Each cluster requires reading the current content, understanding the hub-and-spoke boundaries, and writing specific patches. Combining all 8 into one expansion would produce a sprawling, hard-to-review output that the decider would struggle to integrate. The hub-and-spoke strategy document (EXP-089) already exists. Each cluster is a concrete patch job best handled individually.

kernel_of_truth: The curmudgeon correctly identified that the review had significant redundancy as of April 6. However, the document has been actively maintained since then, and several of the identified clusters have been partially or fully addressed through organic edits. The remaining work is targeted spoke patches, not wholesale restructuring. The hub-and-spoke model is the right architectural pattern — it preserves standalone readability for each tab while eliminating the maintenance burden and drift risk of full re-derivations.
